description: The provided image is a non-scientific placeholder icon labeled "Check for updates" and does not display any discernible scientific content, sub-panels, micrographs, spectra, curves, or quantitative data related to the zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel study. Consequently, no material system, sample designation, processing condition, phase morphology, or microstructural feature can be directly observed or verified from the pixel content. The accompanying text extraction summary references multiple experimental figures such as SEM-BSE micrographs revealing bimodal grain structures, EBSD orientation and KAM maps, grain size distribution histograms, and tensile true stress-strain curves for Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50 steels, but the specific identity and scientific content of the current figure remain indeterminate from the visual input. Without a visible caption or identifiable sub-figures, no trends in grain size evolution, ZrC precipitate distribution, strain partitioning behavior, or strength-ductility synergy can be extracted from this image alone. Therefore, the figure in its present state cannot substantiate the paper conclusions regarding heterogeneous grain structure design or deformation mechanisms.

This figure presents backscattered electron (BSE) micrographs showing the pre-deformation microstructures of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with varying zirconium contents. Panel (a) displays the Zr0 steel, exhibiting a homogeneous microstructure composed entirely of coarse, equiaxed FCC grains averaging approximately 12 μm, indicating full recrystallization after annealing at 900°C for 5 min. Panel (b) shows the Zr0.01 steel, which consists predominantly of coarse FCC grains but with a minor fraction of fine FCC grains appearing locally (delineated by a cyan dashed line). Panel (c) reveals the Zr0.50 steel, characterized by a distinct heterogeneous bimodal architecture comprising coarse FCC grain zones interspersed with fine FCC grain zones (separated by cyan dashed lines) at a 50 μm scale. The higher-magnification inset (c1) of the boxed region C1 in (c) resolves the fine FCC domain in detail, showing densely packed equiaxed grains on the order of ~1 μm at a 10 μm scale. Collectively, the images demonstrate that increasing Zr addition progressively suppresses recrystallization via solute drag and ZrC precipitation, transforming the microstructure from a uniform coarse-grained morphology into a heterogeneous coarse-plus-fine dual-phase FCC structure that underpins strength-ductility synergy through hetero-deformation induced strengthening.

Figure 2 presents EBSD characterization of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels prior to deformation, comprising inverse pole figure (IPF), image quality (IQ), and kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps for three alloy compositions. Panels (a)-(a2) for the Zr0 steel show equiaxed FCC grains with low KAM values, indicating a homogeneous fully recrystallized microstructure. Panels (b)-(b2) for the Zr0.01 steel reveal refined recrystallized FCC grains with random orientations and uniformly low KAM values. Panels (c)-(c2) for the Zr0.50 steel display a distinct heterogeneous structure containing coarse-grain (CG) zones and fine-grain (FG) zones; the CG zones maintain low KAM values while the FG zones exhibit markedly elevated KAM values, which caption states indicates a high density of dislocations decorated in these fine FCC grains. The comparison demonstrates that micro-alloyed Zr progressively refines grain size, and higher Zr addition induces partial recrystallization, producing a bimodal grain structure that enables hetero-deformation induced strengthening.

Figure 3 comprises a bar chart (a) and three histograms (b–d) quantifying the FCC grain structure in Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels subjected to the same thermomechanical treatment (80 % cold-rolling and annealing at 900 °C for 5 min, as reported in the text). Panel (a) shows the area fraction of coarse-grain (CG) and fine-grain (FG) zones: Zr0 is essentially all CG (~100 %); Zr0.01 contains a small FG fraction; and Zr0.50 consists of roughly 65 % CG and 35 % FG. Panels (b)–(d) display FCC grain-size distributions. Zr0 exhibits a homogeneous unimodal distribution peaking near 10–11 μm. Zr0.01 shows a finer unimodal distribution peaking near 6–7 μm. Zr0.50 displays a bimodal distribution with peaks near 1 μm and 5 μm, evidencing distinct CG and FG populations. These results indicate that increasing Zr content transforms the microstructure from homogeneous coarse FCC grains into a heterogeneous bimodal architecture, attributed to Zr-retarded recrystallization, which provides the microstructural foundation for the observed strength–ductility synergy.

Figure 4 integrates engineering stress-strain curves and post-mortem fracture surface SEM micrographs to elucidate the strength-ductility synergy in Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Panel (a) presents engineering stress-strain curves for Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50 steels, revealing monotonic increases in yield strength from approximately 435 MPa to 505 MPa, ultimate tensile strength from approximately 820 MPa to 855 MPa, and fracture elongation from approximately 52% to 58% with increasing Zr content. The inset displays the true stress-strain curve of Zr0.50, where the true ultimate tensile strength exceeds 1100 MPa at a true strain near 0.45. Panels (b)-(d) show SEM fracture surfaces of Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50, respectively. The Zr0 fracture surface exhibits relatively small, shallow dimples alongside occasional cleavage-like facets and large voids. The Zr0.01 surface shows a denser, more uniform dimpled rupture morphology. In Zr0.50, the dimples become markedly larger and deeper with prominent tear ridges, indicating superior microvoid coalescence and fracture ductility. These observations collectively support that Zr addition simultaneously elevates strength and ductility, where the enhanced strain hardening in Zr0.50, driven by HDI hardening and deformation twinning as its true UTS exceeds the critical twinning stress, is consistent with the toughening fracture morphology.

Figure 5 presents an EBSD-IQ map and quasi in-situ μ-DIC von Mises strain maps of the hetero-structured Zr0.50 steel (Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel with 0.50 wt% Zr). Panel (a) is an EBSD-IQ map at 0% global strain, revealing the measured area comprising coarse FCC (CG) and fine FCC (FG) zones with a scale bar of 20 μm. Panels (b) and (c) show von Mises strain distributions at 2% and 8% global strain, respectively, using a color scale from 0 to 12. At 2% global strain, significant strain partitioning occurs: the CG zones and hetero-interfaces between FG and CG zones experience high local strain (red/yellow regions), whereas the FG zones remain nearly undeformed (blue/purple regions), consistent with the reported hardness contrast between soft CG and hard FG regions. As the global strain increases to 8%, the local strain within FG zones becomes more pronounced, yet the strain partitioning between soft and hard regions persists. This heterogeneous strain distribution generates geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), promoting heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) strengthening and additional strain hardening, which underlies the strength-ductility synergy in this alloy.

This figure presents TEM and HRTEM characterization of the tensile deformation microstructure in a Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (Zr0.50). Subfigure (a) is a bright-field TEM image revealing planar dislocations in the FCC grain interior at an early deformation stage, with an inset SAED pattern along the <110> FCC zone axis. Subfigure (b) shows severe deformation near the fracture surface characterized by dense slip bands along {111} planes, with another <110> FCC SAED pattern. Subfigures (c) and (c1) document deformation nanotwinning: (c) is a TEM image of a nanotwin region containing multiple nanotwins (~20 nm) marked by red arrows, with a composite SAED pattern showing superimposed <110> FCC and <110> Twin zones; (c1) is an atomic-resolution HRTEM image resolving the coherent twin boundary separating FCC matrix and twin. Subfigures (d) and (d1) illustrate precipitation strengthening by nanoscale ZrC particles: (d) is a TEM image showing dislocations interacting with ~30 nm ZrC particles distributed in the FCC matrix; (d1) provides an FFT pattern indexed as <110> ZrC and an atomic-resolution HRTEM lattice image confirming the ZrC carbide structure. Collectively, these images capture the transition from dislocation-mediated plasticity to nanotwinning and ZrC particle pinning, elucidating the multi-mechanism origin of the strength-ductility synergy in this heterogeneous-grain-structured steel.

Figure 7 presents the thermodynamic equilibrium phase fraction calculations for the Zr0.50 steel (Fe-25Mn-7Al-0.9C-0.50Zr) as a function of temperature from 500 to 1000 °C, computed using Thermo-Calc software. The left panel shows that the FCC phase (red curve) dominates at high temperature, increasing from ~0.65 at 500 °C and stabilizing near 1.0 above ~700 °C. Meanwhile, the BCC phase (blue) starts at ~0.22 but rapidly diminishes to zero around 550 °C, and the κ carbide (purple) exhibits a minor low-temperature peak before disappearing by ~700 °C. The ZrC phase (green) remains nearly invisible on the main scale. The right panel provides a magnified view (0–0.010 fraction) between ~850 and 1000 °C, revealing that the ZrC fraction stabilizes at ~0.006 (0.6 %). These calculations support that after annealing at 900 °C, the steel consists of a single-phase FCC matrix with a small equilibrium fraction of nanoscale ZrC precipitates, which generate Zener pinning to hinder grain boundary migration during partial recrystallization and promote the formation of a heterogeneous grain structure.
